dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for a male with breathing problems-he's in critical condition at [COMPANY_NAME].
I'm Evan, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He was found in severe distress with heavy sweating. He mentioned chest pain but couldn't say much else due to breathing issues.
Can you squeeze my hand and follow me with your eyes for a moment, sir?
He opened his eyes fully in response to my hand movement.
Tell me the county we're in, if you can.
[CITY].
What date is it today?
July 29th.
Please squeeze my finger as hard as you can now.
Airway is currently patent, no signs of obstruction, and tripoding position is noted.
Patient is in severe respiratory distress, using significant accessory muscles, and has audible rales.
Respiratory rate is about 65 and very shallow, indicating acute worsening.
Heart rate is 139-clearly tachycardic as a result of distress.
Patient has perioral and central cyanosis, with heavy sweating noted on the torso and limbs.
He is clearly working very hard to breathe, with signs of extreme difficulty in air movement.
SpO₂ is 46%-indicating critical oxygenation in his airway.
Pulse is strong, tachycardic, and peripheral pitting edema is seen bilaterally in the lower extremities.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15-alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
Sir, may I confirm your name for the record?
What medications do you currently take?
Aspirin.
That scar on your sternum-was it related to heart surgery?
Yes, some operation was done in the chest-cardiac-related.
Any allergies or chronic health issues beyond high blood pressure?
No, but I do have high blood pressure.
When was the last time you ate or drank anything?
Two hours ago at most.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is your chest pain right now?
10-feels sharp and centered here.
Does it move or change with activity, or is there a trigger that makes it worse?
Does the pain travel to your jaw, arm, or back?
It goes up into my jaw-no other locations.
Respiration rate is now at 46-still very high and concerning.
Heart rate is 142-tachycardia persists despite interventions.
Blood pressure is 164/130-severe hypertension is present.
SpO₂ rose slightly to 56%-some improvement with oxygen interventions.
Glucose test showed a reading of 0-likely device failure or a low reading.
EKG reading: sinus tachycardia at 142 BPM, no clear acute infarct detectable.
Placing 4-lead ECG electrode on his chest for continuous monitoring.
12-lead ECG performed-findings indicate possible infarct in the inferoapical region.
Left atrial enlargement noted and possible atrial flutter-documented in the EKG interpretation.
Lasix, 40mg IV, prepared and administered for suspected pulmonary fluid management.
Intravenous line started in left arm-verified patent with blood return.
IV line secured with multiple layers of tape due to the patient's diaphoresis.
NRB at 15L initiated for immediate oxygenation.
4-lead monitoring re-established to maintain ECG tracking for the duration of transport.
Respiratory rate is 45-no significant change, with rales still present in upper lung fields.
Heart rate is 147-continued concern for tachycardia exceeding safe ranges.
SpO₂ has improved to 72%-still critically low but shows minor progress.
BP is 159/116-elevated and within severe compensatory range.
Following the pulmonary edema protocol-Lasix and O2 initiated immediately.
Monitoring IV line, O2 and EKG every 2 minutes-maintained stable so far.
Heart rate is now 151-continued rise is concerning.
EKG line is stable-still no hypotension, stable for now.
RR is now 49 again-raised to 6 L NC to increase O2 supply.
Pain level is 10-remains unchanging and centrally located.
No other radiation than the jaw-no change noted in pain pattern.
Destination is [COMPANY_NAME]-prepare for rapid movement due to patient deterioration.
Patient is being moved with IV locked and sheet for safety due to weakness.
IV maintained, EKG monitoring active-transitioning to NRB for transport.
No clothes or identifiable items-worn only boxer briefs.
Insurance or family not available-he is in full medical custody at present.
Critical 63-year-old male with acute hypoxia and unresponsive to CPAP attempts.
Lasix given IV. O2 via NRB and SpO₂ at 72%. Continued hypoxia.
EKG findings show inferoapical Q-waves and possible left atrial enlargement.
Chest pain 10-multiple attempts with CPAP all failed due to patient refusal and agitation.
Significant distress and cyanosis-initial NRB poorly tolerated before partial improvement.
ED must prepare for rapid stabilization due to critical OD signs and failed CPAP.
Positive response to Lasix-may indicate acute pulmonary edema control.
Patient exhibits elevated systolic pressure with poor diastolic-compensatory response ongoing.
Full IV lock in place and secured-currently on non-rebreather mask.
No documented allergies and no recent interventions beyond EKG and IV per protocols.
ED must anticipate need for catheterization or advanced intervention upon arrival.
Oxygen saturation still suboptimal-SpO₂ at 73% with high effort breathing.
Patient is hypoxic and incapable of providing full Hx-minimal verbalization.
Suspected pulmonary edema and acute cardiac event active-transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] trauma bay.
No lab results or imaging performed-main intervention Lasix and O2 to ED.
Preparing cath lab if infarct-previous and acute injury both noted in 12-lead.
4-lead monitoring in place-sequentially performed 12-lead in the field as per protocol.
He is acutely unwell but conscious-immediate ED stabilizing intervention crucial.
Monitor IV line and EKG for possible setbacks-stabilizing interventions ongoing.
He is in full ED care-he has no prior paperwork and needs rapid phase-in now.
All protocol steps followed-Lasix, O2, and EKG. ED to continue care.
IV line locked and ready-no active infusions en route, ready for ED continuation.
Patient status remains critical-he needs immediate treatment at arrival to ED.
Grid ready-fully prepared for rapid transfer to emergency room.
NRB in hands only-he removes the mask-manual placement required during transitions.
He is weak and resistant-he will not support CPAP per SC in field.
Status red for ED-make ED aware of high risk of cardiac and pulmonary failure.
RR increased slightly over time-management of distress is paramount on arrival.
SpO₂ at 73%-page perfusionist or interventional team per cardiac findings.
Cardiac history discussed briefly with patient-scar suggests prior surgery.
Transporting with patient on the edge of collapse-no delay acceptable in ED.
Interventions done-time to get him into full ECCU care as apnea is a serious threat.
Record is clean-start from this-one of our highest red flag calls today.
He is currently stable with O2 only-big threat is next change leading to arrest.
Critical case-ED must have hands on him the minute he's wheeled in.
Unprotected apnea threat-he likes to pull off mask-need secure oxygen during handoff.
All 12-Lead steps documented-Lasix and EKG process complete-no remaining deficits.
He is in good watchful hands-no compromising him in transport.
No box missing-full logistical and medical provision fulfilled.
No delays or contamination-patient is in prime state for ED hands to act now.
No stacked risks but high ED readiness-he's a lab mimic and needs EKG, labs, and logging up right away.
AC logical full and evident-he is at full care-goods and bads sent over.
Too many events to repack-he's yours now-good luck and best care.
